title: 'Past Lives,' the Start of Oscar Season, and the Top Five Most Romantic Movies of All Time
format: Sean and Amanda review Celine Song's 'Past Lives,' talk to the director about staging that ending, and each count down their five most romantic movies ever.

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: Celine Song's directorial debut, autobiographical in spirit: Nora and Haesung are childhood friends in Seoul, separated when her family emigrates, who reconnect online 12 years later and then again in person 12 years after that, once she's married to Arthur in New York. It's built around the Korean concept of in-yeon (fated connection) and shot in a plain, unshowy style; the opening scene's mystery pays off in the closing one. Greta Lee, Teo Yoo, and John Magaro star; produced by Christine Vachon's Killer Films.

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: Song on writing the script while on staff at 'Wheel of Time,' the autobiographical distance between herself and Nora, and staging the six-and-a-half-minute closing walk to the Uber on East First Street: which direction each character had to move (toward the past, then toward the future), and the unplanned gust of wind that hit at the right moment.

films.title: You Hurt My Feelings
films.note: Song's answer to the show's closing question about the last great thing she's seen: Nicole Holofcener's film with Julia Louis-Dreyfus, whom she calls the funniest person on earth.

segments.heading: The Top Five Most Romantic Movies of All Time
segments.note: Sean and Amanda's individual countdowns, argued out together

picks.title: Phantom Thread
picks.note: A last-minute swap-in, revisited on a recent London trip with friends: a portrait of a marriage that finds the romance in the everyday.

picks.title: The Age of Innocence
picks.note: A second Daniel Day-Lewis pick after Phantom Thread. Scorsese's most overlooked film, about a couple who don't end up together.

picks.title: Love & Basketball
picks.note: Gina Prince-Bythewood's debut, picked as a natural double feature with Past Lives: two childhood friends whose lives keep intersecting and pulling apart.

picks.title: Moonstruck
picks.note: Loves it, refuses to call it a rom-com, on the grounds that its happy ending is earned with real heartache along the way.

picks.title: Reds
picks.note: Warren Beatty's nearly three-and-a-half-hour epic about Jack Reed and Louise Bryant. A personal pick for him; Amanda and her husband, separately, also watched it early in their own relationship.

picks.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
picks.note: Celine Sciamma's romance, which she thinks owes a debt to the Wong Kar-wai films that follow on this list.

picks.title: Happy Together
picks.note: Wong Kar-wai's Hong Kong-to-Argentina breakup story with Tony Leung. She left In the Mood for Love for Sean on purpose.

picks.title: In the Mood for Love
picks.note: His wife Eileen's favorite Wong Kar-wai film, though she actually prefers 2046.

picks.title: Brief Encounter
picks.note: David Lean's 90-minute film about two married strangers who fall for each other at a train station, adapted from a Noel Coward play. Another one of his wife's favorites.

picks.title: Casablanca
picks.note: Had to be here; its ending speech is echoed directly in Past Lives' own.

picks.title: Before Sunset
picks.note: The best ending of the century, he says, though knowing where Jesse and Celine end up in Before Midnight changes how it plays.
